text: This is a robotic-assisted laparoscopic resection of multiple esophageal diverticula with an extensive mediastinal dissection. The patient is an eighty-four-year-old female with a one-year history of regurgitation, increasingly frequent dysphagia, and choking episodes. She denies any recent weight loss and has an Eckert score of four and a GERD HRQL score of five. The patient and medical record reported no history of previous abdominal surgery. The barium swallow from five years prior showed multiple large esophageal diverticula. There were tertiary contractions of the thoracic esophagus, reversed peristalsis, and delayed emptying. Esophageal manometry showed normal LES basal pressures and elevated post-swallow residual pressures. There was adequate esophageal body motor function to withstand fundoplication.

EGD confirmed the presence of large three-centimeter diverticula in the mid-esophagus and at the GE junction, a four-centimeter hiatal hernia, and significant tortuosity in the mid and distal esophagus. Based on the presence of large mid-body and epiphrenic esophageal diverticula, as well as a non-relaxing LES, an esophageal diverticulaectomy, hellermyotomy, and toupee fundoplication were felt to be most appropriate.

Upon exposure of the upper abdomen, the surgeon encounters evidence of a prior fundoplication. Using an ultrasonic dissector and vessel sealer device, adhesions from the liver to the previous fundoplication are taken down. A circumferential hiatal dissection is performed, revealing remnants of the previous wrap. The first epiphrenic diverticulum is identified and isolated, followed by the identification of the second epiphrenic diverticulum. Endoscopic visualization confirms the location of both diverticula on the right anterolateral distal esophagus. A robotic stapler with a forty-five millimeter white load is positioned across both diverticula, and they are transected at the base and removed.

The surgeon performs an extensive mediastinal dissection to access the midbody diverticulum. The proximal esophageal diverticulum is isolated, and a robotic stapler with a forty-five millimeter white load is positioned across it. Endoscopic visualization confirms the patency of the true esophageal lumen and the isolation of the diverticulum. A second robotic stapler is used to completely transect and remove the proximal diverticulum.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the previous Nissen fundoplication, dividing the stitches and using an ultrasonic dissector to take down adhesions, ultimately reversing the fundoplication.

The surgeon begins the esophageal myotomy two centimeters proximal to the LES on the left anterior surface of the esophagus, avoiding the diverticulectomy staple line. The ultrasonic dissector is used to extend the myotomy seven centimeters proximally in the esophagus and three centimeters distally on the stomach, achieving a total length of ten centimeters. Intraoperative endoscopy confirms a widely patent LES with no esophageal or gastric mucosal injury, as verified by a leak test, and an adequate gastric myotomy length.

The surgeon closes the hiatus by re-approximating the posterior crura using two Ethibond sutures in a figure-of-eight pattern. A Toupee fundoplication is performed by passing the gastric fundus behind the esophagus. Three interrupted Ethibond sutures are placed between the fundus, a slip of the right-sided esophagus, and the right crus, near the previously divided short gastric vessels. Three additional interrupted Ethibond sutures are placed between the greater curve of the stomach, a slip of the left-sided esophagus, and the left crus to complete the Toupee fundoplication.

Repeat intraoperative endoscopy was performed, which revealed a hill grade one valve upon retroflexion and again, no evidence of esophageal or gastric mucosal injury. The patient's hospital course was uncomplicated and she was discharged on post-op day two. The aqueous esophagram showed no leak, complete resection of both epiphranic diverticulum and a small mid-body diverticulum. The patient returned to clinic one month later and reported complete resolution of dysphagia and regurgitation, twenty-nine pounds of weight loss, and minimal residual chest pain, producing an Eckert score of four, primarily due to expected post-operative weight loss, and a GERD HRQL score of five.

In conclusion, robotic assistance may facilitate a high mediastinal dissection allowing for access to hard-to-reach midbody esophageal lesions without the need for thoracotomy. In addition, the ergonomic advantage of the robotic platform may alleviate strain associated with long, reoperative laparoscopic procedures.
